title: Higher Hida theory
speaker: Vincent Pilloni
abstract: Abstract: In usual Hida theory, one constructs a module over weight space whose specialitzation to sufficiently regular weight is a space over classical ordinary modular forms. The goal of higher Hida theory is to construct a perfect complex of modules over (part of) weight space whose specialization to classical *non-regular* weights is a complex which (in favorable circumstances) computes the coherent cohomology of the assoicated Shimura variety in that weight.

proof_stages.label: Nonregular abelian-surface modularity and GSp4 packets
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from the conjectural modularity of abelian surfaces over a totally real field, explains that H^1 is nonregular and hence not directly cohomological on GL4, and uses polarization to pass to local packets for GSp4.

proof_stages.label: Coherent-cohomological realization on GSp4 Shimura varieties
proof_stages.summary: The global packet P(A), GSp4 Shimura varieties, and automorphic vector bundles omega_k are introduced, and the expected coherent-cohomology degrees and multiplicities are described as the automorphic source of the desired integral structures.

proof_stages.label: Classical and higher Hida control theorems
proof_stages.summary: After fixing p-levels and the Iwasawa algebras Lambda_2 and Lambda_1, the speaker states classical Hida theory over the full two-dimensional weight space and the higher Hida theorem producing a one-variable perfect complex interpolating ordinary cohomology in degrees 0 and 1 along the boundary weight line.

proof_stages.label: Ordinary Galois pictures for the two families
proof_stages.summary: The Hecke algebras acting on the classical module and on the higher Hida complex are related to GSp4-valued Galois representations, whose distinct inertial shapes explain why the classical ordinary family fails at l=2 while the higher family remains de Rham there.

proof_stages.label: Interpolation sheaves on p-rank strata
proof_stages.summary: The Hida objects are constructed as ordinary cohomology of interpolation sheaves over p-adic completions of p-rank strata: the ordinary locus gives a GL2(Z_p)-reduced torsor and a two-variable sheaf, while the p-rank-at-least-one locus gives the one-variable higher Hida complex.

proof_stages.label: Finiteness, nilpotence, and exact control
proof_stages.summary: A mod-p excision sequence compares open-stratum cohomology with classical coherent cohomology, a key Hecke operator acts nilpotently on supersingular support, and this yields perfectness and, via overconvergent cohomology and analytic continuation, the precise control theorem.

proof_stages.label: Galois-side consequences via density and doubling
proof_stages.summary: The asserted local structure of the Galois representations is proved by density of cohomological automorphic points in the classical family and, for the higher family, by a mod-p weight-(2,2) generalized doubling argument producing two unramified lines or blocks.

examples.name: Elliptic-curve modularity as a misleading analogy
examples.note: The abelian-surface conjecture is briefly compared with elliptic-curve modularity to emphasize that, despite appearances, its nonregularity makes the technical situation different.
examples.engagement_reason: elliptic-curve modularity is invoked only as an analogy that the abelian-surface conjecture first appears to generalize

passes.reason: elliptic-curve modularity is a systematic GL2 case invoked only as a tempting but technically misleading analogy
Elliptic-curve modularity is invoked as a familiar but ultimately unhelpful analogy, not as a structurally special object or a falsifying example.
Elliptic-curve modularity is a standard regular modularity case used only as a foil for comparison.
Elliptic-curve modularity is a standard regular GL2 modularity case, invoked here to show the naive analogy with the non-regular abelian-surface problem is misleading.
Elliptic-curve modularity is a standard regular modularity case used only as a misleading comparison, not as an obstruction.
Elliptic-curve modularity is a standard regular modularity paradigm used only as a misleading foil, not as a structurally special object or disproof.
The dimension-one GL2 modularity base case is used as a foil showing the naive generalization analogy is misleading.
It is invoked only as a superficial analogy that the speaker says is technically misleading, not as a refutation, and it is structurally a standard modularity setting.
used as a foil to reject the naive elliptic-curve-modularity analogy, while elliptic-curve modularity itself is a standard GL2 case
Elliptic-curve modularity is invoked only as a contrast/misleading analogy, and is a standard modularity theorem rather than a structurally special object here.
It is used as a foil showing the elliptic-curve modularity analogy is misleading, while elliptic-curve modularity is otherwise a standard regular GL2 case.
Used as a heuristic foil rather than to refute a mathematical claim, and elliptic-curve modularity is a systematic typical phenomenon.

examples.name: Artin conjecture for odd two-dimensional representations
examples.note: Artin's conjecture is invoked as another nonregular automorphic/Galois problem, illustrating why the abelian-surface case should be thought of through nonregular methods rather than as a routine elliptic-curve generalization.
examples.engagement_reason: Artin’s conjecture for odd two-dimensional representations is cited only as the closer technical analogue because of non-regularity

passes.reason: odd two-dimensional Artin representations form a systematic nonregular class used as a positive technical analogy rather than to refute a claim
Odd two-dimensional Artin representations are used as the better non-regular analogue, within a systematic family rather than a sporadic or extremal case.
Odd two-dimensional Artin representations are invoked as a non-regular collapsed-Hodge-weight situation analogous to the abelian-surface case.
Odd two-dimensional Artin representations form a non-regular finite-image/weight-one situation with collapsed Hodge-Tate weights, used as a positive technical analogy rather than a refutation.
Odd two-dimensional Artin representations form a systematic nonregular analogy rather than a sporadic or boundary object, and are not used negatively.
Odd two-dimensional Artin representations have collapsed/non-regular Hodge--Tate structure, which is exactly why they are compared to the abelian-surface case.
Odd two-dimensional Artin representations give a non-regular finite-image case cited as the positive technical analogue, not as a disproof.
It is used as a non-regular analogue rather than an adversarial example, and Artin/weight-one two-dimensional representations have collapsed repeated Hodge--Tate weight structure.
invoked as a positive analogue of a non-regular motive with collapsed/repeated Hodge–Tate weights
Artin’s conjecture for odd two-dimensional representations is used as an analogous non-regular situation, not to refute a claim, and is structurally ordinary within that setting.
It is invoked as the more apt analogue because odd two-dimensional Artin motives are non-regular, with collapsed/repeated Hodge--Tate weight data.
Invoked as a technical analogy for non-regular motives, not as a failure case, and odd two-dimensional Artin representations form a standard infinite class.

examples.name: Paramodular conjecture and canonical new vector
examples.note: A special choice of holomorphic components at infinity and generic finite packet members gives the familiar paramodular weight-two cusp form, illustrating the GSp4 packet/coherent-cohomology setup without being needed for the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker spells out the paramodular-conjecture choice of packet member and constructs its canonical line from lowest-weight and paramodular new vectors, yielding a weight-two cusp form

passes.reason: the canonical line is generated by a lowest-weight vector and a paramodular new vector at minimal new level
The canonical cusp form is generated by lowest-weight and paramodular new vectors, hence by minimal/lowest-level extremal choices, and is not used to refute a claim.
The canonical paramodular new vector is distinguished by minimal paramodular level and lowest-weight choices.
The canonical paramodular vector is a minimal-level newvector/lowest-weight line, and in weight two uses holomorphic limits of discrete series, so it is boundary-like but not a counterexample.
The canonical paramodular new vector is distinguished by minimal conductor/lowest-weight data and is used constructively to produce a weight-two cusp form.
The paramodular form is built from weight-two holomorphic limit-of-discrete-series data, while the canonical new vector is minimal-level/lowest-weight in the newform sense.
The paramodular form uses boundary weight-two limit-of-discrete-series data and a lowest-weight/minimal-level new vector, without serving as a counterexample.
It is explanatory rather than refutational, and the canonical new vector is distinguished by minimal conductor/level and lowest-weight data.
the distinguished paramodular form uses limit-of-discrete-series weight-two boundary data and a lowest-weight minimal-level new vector
The paramodular new vector is used to exhibit the canonical weight-two cusp form, and the new vector is extremal as the minimal-level/conductor vector in its representation.
The paramodular new vector is a canonical minimal-level/new vector, so it is structurally extremal but not used to refute a claim.
Presented as the expected canonical automorphic realization, not a counterexample, with the new vector structurally extremal by having minimal paramodular level/conductor.
